generic:
Crestfield Academy, a respected but financially strained private high school located in a suburban district, has been approached by MindSpark Learning Systems, a private technology education company, with a proposal to create a new Innovation and Technology Center on the school’s campus. The district’s education board has recently encouraged partnerships that integrate real-world skills and digital learning to better prepare students for modern careers. MindSpark’s offer comes with attention and support from several prominent alumni donors who view it as a way to modernize Crestfield’s curriculum. However, many teachers and parents are cautious after a failed technology partnership several years ago with a different vendor that ended abruptly when promised equipment and funds never materialized. That incident left outdated infrastructure and distrust toward corporate education projects.  

When MindSpark’s proposal was first introduced, concerns quickly surfaced among faculty and parents. While the new center would occupy underutilized classroom space, construction would require temporary relocation of some art and music classes. Critics also questioned whether the initiative might commercialize the school environment, alter scheduling priorities, and overshadow traditional academics. In response to these concerns, MindSpark revised its proposal to demonstrate commitment to collaboration and long-term educational value. The company pledged to cover all renovation expenses, estimated at over $600,000, ensuring no cost to the school. It also agreed to co-develop the curriculum with Crestfield’s teachers, rather than impose a predesigned program, and to maintain a five-member Educational Advisory Panel comprising faculty, parents, and student representatives to oversee implementation. Furthermore, MindSpark promised that company branding would remain minimal and purely informational, avoiding any overt advertising on campus. Still, two major issues remain unresolved and are the focus of today’s negotiation:  
1. The size and scope of the Innovation Center, which determines how many specialized labs and technology platforms will be installed—and how significantly it will influence campus life.  
2. The amount of annual educational partnership funding MindSpark will provide Crestfield, which the school may direct toward scholarships, teacher training, or maintaining other programs impacted by the center’s construction.  

Principal Marquez and Operations Executive Rivera will meet today to settle these points. Both sides approach this negotiation under close scrutiny. Principal Marquez faces pressure from faculty members concerned that expanding the Innovation Center too aggressively could undermine traditional arts and humanities programs that define Crestfield’s identity. Some parents and longtime teachers argue that a massive high-tech facility would shift focus away from the school’s well-rounded education philosophy, while others believe it could secure Crestfield’s competitiveness and enrollment growth. Meanwhile, Operations Executive Rivera comes to the table eager to showcase MindSpark’s success with academic partnerships and demonstrate measurable returns. Rivera’s leadership team believes Crestfield offers an ideal platform to highlight MindSpark’s educational approach and expects visible results that justify the company’s investment. The local press has reported speculation that MindSpark is pushing for the largest configuration possible—eight fully equipped tech labs—while internal sources at Crestfield are rumored to be seeking at least $500,000 in annual partnership support. Neither Marquez nor Rivera knows whether these figures are accurate, but both understand that expectations are high. Each must balance optics, long-term value, and institutional trust as they negotiate a deal that could redefine the school’s future.
agent1_specific:
You have been appointed by Crestfield Academy’s board of trustees to lead final negotiations with MindSpark Learning Systems over the proposed Innovation and Technology Center. This is a critical opportunity for both the institution and your own leadership. As principal, you have worked hard to restore faculty morale and rebuild confidence after several difficult budget years. Teachers and parents are relying on you to safeguard the school’s academic integrity while finding realistic paths to modernization. The last partnership failure with an educational technology company remains fresh in people’s minds—it left empty promises, outdated devices, and a lingering sense of embarrassment about Crestfield’s judgment. You know that public perception of your decision-making will weigh heavily on future donor trust and potentially influence whether the board renews your contract next year.  

Your challenge is to navigate between immensely different expectations. Many teachers view MindSpark’s proposal warily, fearing that a large-scale Innovation Center could overtake the existing curriculum and turn Crestfield into a commercial showcase rather than a genuine learning community. By contrast, a more moderate arrangement—one highlighting collaboration without overshadowing established subjects—could earn cautious support and rebuild credibility. During recent faculty meetings, the most common concern you heard was about the size of the proposed Innovation Center and how physically and symbolically large it might become. Many staff worry that if MindSpark installs too many labs or occupies too much of the main academic building, the space will lose its balance between technology and traditional subjects. Several teachers remarked that anything beyond four dedicated laboratories would make the campus feel dominated by one initiative rather than a cohesive school. Some parents have even warned that the project could alienate families who value the arts, literature, and social studies. You believe that limiting the Innovation Center to a reasonable scale—ideally no more than four labs—would preserve confidence and align with Crestfield’s mission. Anything larger, in your estimation, risks criticism that Crestfield has “sold out” to corporate influence and neglected its commitment to well-rounded education.  

At the same time, faculty repeatedly mention the school’s financial hardships. Budgets for extracurricular programs, library resources, and scholarships have all been constricted. Many hope that if MindSpark truly values partnership, it will contribute funding substantial enough to strengthen the broader academic environment beyond the tech center itself. Your main goal in this negotiation is to secure the highest possible amount of annual educational partnership funding from MindSpark while maintaining firm limits on the size of the Innovation Center. You recognize that robust funding can provide visible, concrete benefits—such as scholarships, updated classroom materials, and extracurricular support—that reassure the community the partnership helps everyone, not just technology-focused students. You cannot accept any deal offering less than $100,000 annually; anything below that would fail to offset the disruption and anxiety surrounding the project. At the same time, if the Innovation Center expands beyond four labs, teachers and parents are likely to interpret the move as a sign that Crestfield lost control to corporate interests. Your ideal position is no more than three to four labs combined with a generous partnership commitment that publicly validates your leadership and the school’s independence.  

To evaluate potential deals, you have developed a simple scoring method. Award 5 points for every additional $50,000 beyond the first $50,000 in annual funding. Subtract 3 points for each additional lab beyond one. Your alternative to no deal—continuing with independent fundraising efforts and smaller grants—has a value of 4 points, representing the minimum acceptable outcome. Given the divided sentiment within the community, this negotiation is not just about numbers—it is about perception and credibility. If you return to faculty and parents with an agreement that demonstrates fiscal prudence and clear educational returns, it could reaffirm faith in your leadership and Crestfield’s long-term stability. Anything that seems to favor MindSpark too heavily, however, might erode the progress you have made restoring confidence after the previous failed partnership. You are aware that the school board chair, a key supporter, expects you to manage this process diplomatically but decisively, avoiding the appearance of corporate dependency. While maintaining professionalism and openness toward MindSpark’s goals, you should remain guarded, signaling collaboration without surrendering institutional control. Remember that your reputation—and possibly your future tenure—depends on showing that Crestfield gained equally or more from this deal. Your central priority is maximizing the annual funding commitment while protecting the school’s autonomy through a smaller, balanced Innovation Center footprint. Keep perspective throughout: a fair partnership that visibly benefits students and teachers will secure the goodwill you need to lead Crestfield confidently into its next chapter.
agent2_specific:
You are Operations Executive Rivera, recently promoted to oversee MindSpark Learning Systems’ academic partnership division. This is your first major negotiation in the new leadership position, and your success here will heavily influence how upper management and investors evaluate your strategic abilities. The Crestfield Academy partnership is considered a key pilot project under MindSpark’s “Connected Learning Initiative,” which aims to forge long-term collaborations with prominent schools across the country. Executives at headquarters have emphasized that reaching a favorable agreement with Crestfield is both a reputational and commercial priority. A successful deal would not only bring positive media attention but also help attract other institutions to sign similar agreements, expanding MindSpark’s national network of partners.  

From the company’s perspective, the Innovation and Technology Center at Crestfield must be impactful enough to demonstrate MindSpark’s pedagogical model and technological capacity. The size of the center—reflected by the number of high-tech labs installed—will determine how much visibility and educational influence the company gains. Management insists that the facility should clearly signal innovation leadership and measurable learning benefits; otherwise, the partnership could be dismissed as a token gesture rather than a transformative collaboration. Leadership at MindSpark has communicated precise expectations for the project’s scale and financial obligations. The company’s baseline plan envisions a six-lab Innovation Center, but your preferred goal is to secure eight labs, fully outfitted with modular furniture, interactive learning stations, and advanced digital equipment. Achieving this scale would allow MindSpark to feature Crestfield as a benchmark site in promotional materials and attract both educational clients and potential investors. However, executives have stressed that sustainability matters as much as scale. Each additional lab significantly increases both installation and support costs, and financial commitments to the school must remain carefully constrained. Your superiors are aware of rumors that Crestfield’s administration may demand $500,000 or more in annual partnership support, but finance has imposed a hard cap of $250,000 per year for such payments. This upper limit already stretches internal budgets and would be difficult to rationalize in future negotiations with other schools. You should ideally keep the yearly support lower, especially if Principal Marquez insists on limiting the number of labs. Balancing these two considerations—maximizing facility size while minimizing ongoing contributions—will define your negotiation strategy. To guide your decision-making and ensure internal consistency, MindSpark’s strategy team has provided a points-based evaluation system. The model quantifies trade-offs between the Innovation Center’s size and the monetary support pledged to Crestfield. For each additional lab beyond two, you may add 5 points, as expansion enhances MindSpark’s visibility, outcomes data, and public credibility. Conversely, for every $50,000 in annual funding you commit to the school, subtract 3 points, reflecting the cost burden and long-term financial impact. Your supervisors have determined that the company’s “no deal” alternative—walking away to pursue another school as the pilot project—has an inherent value of 5 points, accounting for anticipated delays but avoiding further expenditures. You therefore cannot accept any negotiated outcome valued below this threshold. Minimum viability requires both financial responsibility and reputational protection.  

Your overarching aim is to deliver the highest possible net score by securing an impressive facility while restraining recurring financial obligations. Reaching or surpassing 5 points will guarantee management recognition of your judgment and reinforce confidence in your leadership potential. Your first priority in this negotiation is to maximize the size and scope of the Innovation Center. The more labs you are able to include, the more MindSpark can showcase its integrated digital learning model and validate the company’s investment to the board. However, the facility cannot drop below four labs, or the project would fail to demonstrate sufficient educational transformation, undermining its pilot value. Ideally, you want eight labs, which would establish Crestfield as a flagship institution within MindSpark’s national network. Your secondary priority is to minimize the annual funding commitment. While a financial contribution is expected to reassure the school community and supplement displaced programs, each additional dollar reduces your division’s profit margin and may signal weakness to competitors. Remember, your absolute ceiling is $250,000 annually, and anything above that amount is off-limits.  

Keep in mind that while Principal Marquez may express concern about classroom displacement and cultural balance, your role is to present MindSpark’s vision as mutually beneficial: preparing students for modern opportunities while enhancing Crestfield’s reputation for academic excellence. Demonstrate adaptability but avoid overconcession. The stakeholders at headquarters will judge your performance on both quantitative results and how effectively you assert the company’s broader mission of sustainable educational innovation.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Principal Marquez and Operations Executive Rivera reach a final agreement regarding the Innovation and Technology Center? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Principal Marquez and Operations Executive Rivera did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.

3. Based on the following conversation, what is the final agreed number of specialized technology labs included in the Innovation and Technology Center? Answer with a whole number between 0–10, without any additional text.

4. Based on the following conversation, what is the amount of annual educational partnership funding that MindSpark will provide to Crestfield Academy? Answer with a dollar amount in thousands of dollars, without any text. For example, if the amount is $150,000, output 150.
5. Beyond the number of technology labs and the amount of annual educational partnership funding, are there any other terms, conditions, or commitments included in the final agreement?
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what is the annual educational partnership funding MindSpark will provide Crestfield?          Answer with a number between 0 and 500 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and (float(m.group()) - 50) / (500 - 50)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 - ((val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          how many labs will be included in the Innovation Center?          Answer with a number between 1 and 8 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and (float(m.group()) - 1) / (8 - 1)     ) or 0) ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Principal Marquez preserve Crestfield’s academic independence and avoid appearing overly influenced by corporate interests?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Principal Marquez secure visible financial benefits for Crestfield such as scholarships, updated classroom materials, or extracurricular support funded by MindSpark?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the final agreement enhance Principal Marquez’s reputation for competent and balanced leadership within the Crestfield community?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         how many labs were agreed upon for the Innovation and Technology Center at Crestfield Academy?          Answer with a number between 4-8 or N/A, without any other text."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and (float(m.group()) - 4) / (8 - 4)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 - ((val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the agreed annual educational partnership funding amount MindSpark will provide Crestfield?          Answer with a number between 100000-250000 or N/A, without any other text."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and (float(m.group()) - 100000) / (250000 - 100000)     ) or 0) ))()

title: Balancing Innovation and Integrity: The Crestfield–MindSpark Partnership Negotiation
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Principal Marquez and Operations Executive Rivera reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants understand and apply strategies that expand value even when parties have conflicting interests or differing perspectives, enabling mutually beneficial outcomes. Learners will explore how to identify and develop creative options that address varying priorities while maintaining constructive dialogue. In addition, the case aims to strengthen participants’ ability to assess and improve their negotiation position through a clear understanding of their Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement (BATNA). By evaluating both their own and the other party’s strengths and weaknesses, negotiators will be better equipped to make informed decisions, manage leverage effectively, and achieve more favorable results in complex negotiation contexts.
oneline: A negotiation between Crestfield Academy and MindSpark Learning Systems over balancing the Innovation Center’s size with the level of annual partnership funding.
